unit.title: Script And Sound Survival
unit.communicationGoal: Recognize the key beginner sounds of the Kyrgyz Cyrillic alphabet, read high-value words in Cyrillic, and respond with short social-survival phrases.
pronunciationFocus: Open vowel А as a stable, familiar anchor sound
Front rounded vowels Ө and Ү as a sound family distinct from О and У
The single nasal consonant Ң written as one Cyrillic letter
The back fricative in Рахмат rendered as kh in transliteration

lexemes.notes: A solid anchor letter. It appears clearly in high-frequency words like апа (mother) and helps learners trust the script early.
A front rounded vowel. If you know Turkish or German, it is close to the oe or o-umlaut sound. It belongs to the front-vowel harmony group.
Another front rounded vowel, paired with Ө. Words like үй (house) show it in a real beginner context. It often co-occurs with other front vowels due to vowel harmony.
One sound, not two. Pronounce it like the ng in sing. It is common in the middle and end of Kyrgyz words.
A high-value survival word from day one. The long oo sound is worth hearing and practicing early. Back-vowel harmony pattern.
The first consonant Ж is softer than English j and often rendered as zh in transliteration. Essential from the very first lesson.
A casual, friendly greeting. Short enough to practice reading as a whole chunk. Back-vowel harmony pattern.
Common across Central Asia. The kh in the middle signals a deeper back-of-mouth sound rather than plain English h.
Polite form. The ending -сиз is a polite second-person marker. Use freely in public settings when navigating or asking for help.
Contains the special Ң letter in the middle. A good early example of Ң in a real word rather than an abstract drill.

grammarNotes.title: Kyrgyz Cyrillic adds a few letters beyond standard Russian
grammarNotes.summary: You do not need the whole alphabet at once. Focus on the letters that appear in your first few words. The extra Kyrgyz-specific letters — Ө, Ү, and Ң — carry real meaning differences and show up often in beginner vocabulary. Recognizing them early prevents confusion later when you start reading more.

grammarNotes.title: Vowel harmony: front and back vowels travel together
grammarNotes.summary: Kyrgyz vowels divide into two families. Back vowels — А, О, У — travel together. Front vowels — Е, Ө, Ү, И — travel together. When a word has back vowels, suffixes also take back-vowel forms. When a word has front vowels, suffixes take front-vowel forms. You do not need the full rule set yet, but noticing that Ө and Ү belong to the front-vowel family now will help you hear suffix patterns later.

tasks.objective: Read short Kyrgyz words in Cyrillic and connect each one to its sound and meaning.
tasks.instructions: Look at the Kyrgyz word first and try to read it aloud. Use the transliteration only when you need a quick pronunciation check. Then match the word to its meaning.
successCriteria: Read at least one word with А aloud without looking at the transliteration.
Recognize a word containing Ө or Ү by sight.
Connect Ооба and Жок to their meanings without translating letter by letter.
Spot the Ң letter in Жаңы and say it as one ng sound.
tasks.completionHint: Read the overall word shape first, then zoom into the special letters.

supportCards.title: Special letters now, suffix patterns later
supportCards.body: When you notice Ө or Ү in a word, you are not just memorizing a symbol. You are training your eye for a front-vowel pattern that shapes suffixes all through the language.

supportCards.title: Try the Cyrillic first, transliteration second
supportCards.body: Read each word once in Cyrillic before peeking at the Latin. Even two seconds of independent reading builds script confidence much faster than skipping straight to transliteration.

supportCards.title: Жаңы is a great Ң test word
supportCards.body: Жаңы means new. The letter Ң sits right in the middle, making it easy to spot. Once you nail the ng sound here, you will recognize it quickly in longer words later.

reviewSeeds.prompt: Which Kyrgyz letter writes the single ng sound as in 'sing'?
How do you say 'excuse me' in Kyrgyz?
